Suppose we take these claims as describing a system that searches conceptual space.

Its progress cannot be pictured as a simple narrowing toward one answer. If creative progress alternates expansion and compression, then at some stages the system must deliberately widen the range of candidate ideas, and at other stages it must prune them. So any account of its success has to include both phases: a loosening that admits more structure, and a tightening that reduces it. “Collapsing search space” is therefore incomplete as a metaphor, because collapse is only half the cycle.

Now add the warning about weirdness. During expansion, the system will generate candidates that depart from familiar conceptual patterns. If it allows too little deviation, then it will remain confined to what human memory already benchmarks; it will merely reproduce or approximate regions already shaped by known examples. If it allows too much deviation, then the expansion phase becomes flooded with attractive but untestable constructions. Compression would then have too little reliable basis for pruning, because seductiveness is not the same as testability.

This sets up the deepest open question. The system may be able to approximate “Einstein-shaped” regions of conceptual space: that is, it may reach forms of explanation recognizably like those humans retrospectively honor as profound, without actually escaping the template library latent in human memory. In that case, alternating expansion and compression would still yield impressive outputs, but only within a space whose benchmarks are already culturally stored. The open question is whether the cycle can do more: whether expansion can reach explanations not benchmarked in human memory, and whether compression can retain them for reasons other than resemblance to prior human exemplars.

The two remaining premises introduce strings with no stated interpretation: “Fibroblast ephesian retent postgenial preallude” and “Inherits pedomotor hydrothorax postcubital scyllarus.” Since all premises are to be treated as true, these strings exist in the world of the thought experiment as valid items, but nothing else in the premises assigns them meaning, testability, explanatory role, or relation to the architecture. Their presence therefore sharpens the weirdness problem. They are candidates for content that may be grammatical, seductive, technical-sounding, or meaningless; from the premises alone, none of these classifications is licensed. A system that tolerates weirdness must decide what to do with such items. If it rejects them merely for lacking familiar benchmarks, it may foreclose discovery beyond human memory. If it admits them too freely, it risks drowning in untestable invention.

So the architecture stands between two failures. One is safe compression into known shapes. The other is uncontrolled expansion into unverifiable novelty. The unresolved issue is whether there exists a disciplined alternation of expansion and compression that can distinguish genuinely new explanation from benchmarkless noise, even when confronted with material that, like those final strings, has no guaranteed place in prior human understanding.
